title: Game Theory & Strategic Thinking
subtitle: Nash equilibrium, mixed strategies, and auction intuition
intro: Game theory models strategic interaction: each player chooses an action knowing that others are doing the same, and payoffs depend on everyone's choices. In quant and trading interviews you'll see games in market making, bidding, and adversarial scenarios where your best move depends on what others do.

A Nash equilibrium is a profile of strategies where no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their move. Sometimes the equilibrium involves mixed strategies — randomizing over actions with specific probabilities so opponents cannot exploit a predictable pattern. Recognizing dominant strategies, prisoner's dilemmas, and zero-sum structure is essential for quick interview reasoning.

Game theory models markets where your payoff depends on others' choices—auctions, bidding wars, and adversarial market making all fit. Quant interviews rarely ask you to solve a general game in full, but they do test Nash equilibrium, mixed strategies, and whether a game is zero-sum. Recognize when a dominant strategy exists, when randomization is optimal, and when bid shading beats bidding your true value.

questions.question: Two market makers simultaneously choose to quote Tight or Wide. The pair of quotes is a Nash equilibrium when:
options: neither maker can increase their own payoff by changing only their own quote
both makers earn exactly the same payoff
one maker always captures the entire spread
the two makers coordinate to maximize their combined payoff

explanations.A: Correct. A Nash equilibrium is a profile where each player's strategy is a best response to the others, so no unilateral switch can raise that player's payoff.
explanations.B: Equilibrium says nothing about equal payoffs; players can earn very different amounts and still sit at a Nash equilibrium.
explanations.C: One side dominating is not the definition. Equilibrium is about the absence of a profitable unilateral deviation, not about who wins.
explanations.D: Jointly maximizing total payoff describes a cooperative or Pareto outcome, which often differs from the Nash equilibrium (as in the prisoner's dilemma).

remediation.question: At a Nash equilibrium, each player's strategy is a best response to:

explanations.A: Correct. Each player's choice maximizes their payoff given what everyone else is doing — that mutual best-response property defines equilibrium.
explanations.B: Strategies are chosen against opponents' play, not in a vacuum.
explanations.C: Players respond to opponents' actual strategies, not arbitrary noise.
explanations.D: A player can't always grab the highest payoff; the best response is constrained by others' choices.

remediation.question: If you can improve your payoff by switching your move alone, the current profile is:

explanations.A: Correct. A profitable unilateral deviation means the profile fails the no-deviation test, so it is not a Nash equilibrium.
explanations.B: It cannot be a Nash equilibrium precisely because a beneficial unilateral switch exists.
explanations.C: If you can do better by deviating, the current profile is not optimal for you.
explanations.D: A dominant strategy is a property of a single action across all opponent moves, not a description of an unstable profile.

questions.question: No matter what your competitor does, quoting Tight earns you more than quoting Wide. For you, quoting Tight is:

explanations.A: Correct. A dominant strategy yields the best payoff regardless of opponents' choices, which is exactly what Tight does here.
explanations.B: A dominated strategy is one you'd never play because something always beats it — that's Wide here, not Tight.
explanations.C: A mixed strategy randomizes over actions; Tight is a single pure action that's always best.
explanations.D: Tight strictly improves your payoff in every case, so it is highly relevant, not irrelevant.

remediation.question: If one action beats all others no matter what opponents choose, it is:

explanations.A: Correct. Beating every alternative against all opponent choices is the definition of a strictly dominant strategy.
explanations.B: Mixed means randomizing; a single always-best action is pure, not mixed.
explanations.C: Pareto optimality compares joint outcomes; it is a different concept from dominance for one player.
explanations.D: Zero-sum describes the payoff structure of a game, not a single action's dominance.

remediation.question: When a player has a strictly dominant strategy, rational play should:

explanations.A: Correct. By definition it yields a strictly higher payoff in every scenario, so a rational player always plays it.
explanations.B: Never choosing the best-in-all-cases action would be irrational.
explanations.C: Randomizing throws away guaranteed gains from the dominant action.
explanations.D: Copying the opponent ignores that you already have an action that's best regardless of their move.

questions.question: Two desks trade only against each other, so every dollar one gains the other loses. Across both desks, the total payoff for any outcome is:

explanations.A: Correct. In a zero-sum (constant-sum) game the payoffs always add to the same total — zero here — because one side's gain is exactly the other's loss.
explanations.B: If totals were always positive, gains wouldn't be offset by equal losses, contradicting zero-sum.
explanations.C: Always-negative totals would mean both sides lose every round, which isn't a pure transfer.
explanations.D: The sum is fixed by construction, not random; only the split between players varies.

remediation.question: Zero-sum means one player's gain equals the other's:

explanations.A: Correct. In a zero-sum game payoffs are a pure transfer: +x to one player is −x to the other.
explanations.B: If both gained, the total would rise above zero, breaking the zero-sum condition.
explanations.C: The transfer is exact, not random.
explanations.D: Zero-sum games are purely competitive; there is no joint gain from cooperation.

remediation.question: Poker heads-up for fixed stakes is often modeled as:

explanations.A: Correct. One player's winnings are the other's losses (ignoring the rake), so the chips form a zero-sum transfer.
explanations.B: Heads-up poker is adversarial, not cooperative.
explanations.C: Poker is intensely strategic — bluffing and bet sizing are strategic choices.
explanations.D: The pot is finite and fixed; payoffs sum to zero, not infinity.

questions.question: In the classic prisoner's dilemma, the Nash equilibrium is:

explanations.A: Correct. Defect is dominant for each player, so mutual defection is the unique Nash equilibrium even though it is worse for both than mutual cooperation.
explanations.B: Mutual cooperation gives a higher joint payoff but isn't stable — each player can gain by unilaterally defecting.
explanations.C: An asymmetric profile isn't an equilibrium because the cooperator could improve by defecting too.
explanations.D: An equilibrium does exist — it is mutual defection.

remediation.question: In prisoner's dilemma, defect is a dominant strategy because:
options: it pays more regardless of the other's choice

explanations.A: Correct. Whether the opponent cooperates or defects, defecting yields a strictly higher payoff for you.
explanations.B: Cooperation only gives a higher joint payoff under mutual cooperation; it is never the individual best response.
explanations.C: Payoffs differ across outcomes; that asymmetry is what creates the dilemma.
explanations.D: Each player has well-defined strategies — cooperate or defect.

remediation.question: Both players cooperating would be better for both, but Nash equilibrium can still be:

explanations.A: Correct. Individually rational defection leads to mutual defection — a Nash equilibrium that's Pareto-dominated by cooperation.
explanations.B: Mutual cooperation isn't a Nash equilibrium because each player can gain by defecting.
explanations.C: The equilibrium here is a pure strategy (defect), not randomization.
explanations.D: Players do act; the equilibrium is mutual defection, not abstention.

questions.question: In matching pennies, the unique Nash equilibrium has each player choosing heads with probability:

explanations.A: Correct. Mixing 50/50 makes the opponent indifferent between heads and tails, the only way neither player can be exploited.
explanations.B: Always playing heads (probability 1) is a pure strategy the opponent can exploit by best-responding.
explanations.C: Never playing heads (probability 0) is equally exploitable as a predictable pure strategy.
explanations.D: 1/4 is not symmetric and leaves a predictable bias the opponent can punish; the equilibrium is exactly 1/2.

remediation.question: A mixed strategy means a player:
options: randomizes over actions with specific probabilities

explanations.A: Correct. A mixed strategy assigns probabilities to actions and randomizes accordingly.
explanations.B: Always playing one action is a pure strategy, not a mixed one.
explanations.C: Refusing to randomize is precisely a pure strategy.
explanations.D: Copying the opponent is a reactive rule, not a probability distribution over your own actions.

remediation.question: In matching pennies, if you always play heads, your opponent can exploit you by always playing:

explanations.A: Correct. A predictable 'always heads' lets the mismatching opponent guarantee a win by always playing tails.
explanations.B: Matching your heads would help you, not exploit you, when the opponent profits from mismatches.
explanations.C: Randomizing is unnecessary once your move is perfectly predictable — a pure counter is strictly better.
explanations.D: Doing nothing forfeits the free exploit your predictability hands the opponent.

questions.question: In a mixed-strategy equilibrium, each player must be indifferent between the actions they mix over, meaning:
options: those actions yield equal expected payoff
payoffs are maximized by one action only

explanations.A: Correct. A player only randomizes if the mixed-over actions give equal expected payoff; otherwise they would shift all weight to the better one.
explanations.B: Indifference means equal expected payoffs, which need not be zero.
explanations.C: If one action strictly maximized payoff, the player would play it purely, not mix.
explanations.D: Randomization is not only possible but required for the mixed equilibrium.

remediation.question: If one mixed action had higher expected payoff, a rational player would:

explanations.A: Correct. A strictly better action would be played purely (probability 1), which breaks the mixing — so equilibrium mixing requires equal expected payoffs.
explanations.B: Never playing the higher-payoff action is the opposite of rational.
explanations.C: Uniform randomizing wastes the edge from the strictly better action.
explanations.D: Quitting forgoes a positive-payoff opportunity.

remediation.question: The indifference condition is used to solve for:

explanations.A: Correct. Setting the opponent's expected payoffs from each action equal lets you solve for the equilibrium mixing probabilities.
explanations.B: Sample size is a statistics concept, unrelated to equilibrium mixing.
explanations.C: Variance is not what the indifference condition pins down.
explanations.D: Confidence intervals come from inference, not from equilibrium solving.

questions.question: In a sequential game, the standard solution concept is:

explanations.A: Correct. You solve sequential games by backward induction — reasoning from the final decisions back to the first move.
explanations.B: Random play ignores the structure that sequential information gives you.
explanations.C: Ignoring later moves discards exactly the information backward induction uses.
explanations.D: Rational players maximize their own payoff, not the opponent's.

remediation.question: Backward induction starts analyzing the game from:

explanations.A: Correct. You solve the final subgames first, then fold those optimal choices back toward the root.
explanations.B: Starting only at the first move ignores how later decisions constrain earlier ones.
explanations.C: The method follows the game tree's order, not random nodes.
explanations.D: It works from terminal decisions, not from arbitrary zero payoffs.

remediation.question: A subgame perfect equilibrium rules out:

explanations.A: Correct. Subgame perfection requires optimal play in every subgame, eliminating threats a player would not actually carry out.
explanations.B: It refines, not rules out, Nash equilibria — every subgame perfect equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium.
explanations.C: It enforces rational play in every subgame rather than ruling it out.
explanations.D: Mixed strategies are still allowed within subgame perfect equilibria.

questions.question: In a first-price sealed-bid auction, the highest bidder wins and pays:

explanations.A: Correct. In a first-price sealed-bid auction the winner pays exactly the amount they bid.
explanations.B: Paying the second-highest bid describes a second-price (Vickrey) auction, not first-price.
explanations.C: The winner does pay — first-price auctions are not free.
explanations.D: Winners pay their own bid, not an average across all bidders.

remediation.question: In a second-price (Vickrey) auction, the winner pays:

explanations.A: Correct. The highest bidder wins but pays the second-highest bid, which makes truthful bidding a dominant strategy.
explanations.B: Paying your own bid is the first-price rule, not Vickrey.
explanations.C: The winner pays the runner-up's bid, not zero.
explanations.D: There is no doubling — payment equals the second-highest bid.

remediation.question: Bidding your true value is a dominant strategy in a:

explanations.A: Correct. In a second-price auction your payment depends on others' bids, so bidding your true value can never hurt and is dominant.
explanations.B: In a first-price auction bidding true value yields zero surplus when you win, so rational bidders shade below value.
explanations.C: A lottery has no strategic bidding of values.
explanations.D: Truthful bidding being dominant is an auction-format property, not a zero-sum property.

questions.question: In matching pennies, Row wins if both match (HH or TT), Column wins if they differ (HT or TH). This game is:

explanations.A: Correct. Exactly one player wins each round and the other loses, so payoffs sum to zero — it is a zero-sum game.
explanations.B: The players' interests are strictly opposed, the opposite of cooperative.
explanations.C: No single strategy dominates; that is why the equilibrium is mixed.
explanations.D: The game is highly strategic — each player tries to outguess the other.

remediation.question: Matching pennies has no pure-strategy Nash equilibrium because:
options: either player wants to mismatch the other's predictable choice

explanations.A: Correct. Against any pure choice one player can always profit by reacting, so no pure profile is stable — there is no pure-strategy equilibrium.
explanations.B: Payoffs are not equal across outcomes; one side wins and the other loses.
explanations.C: Players don't always match; Column specifically wants to mismatch.
explanations.D: Each player has two actions (heads or tails), not one.

remediation.question: The expected payoff in the mixed equilibrium of matching pennies is:

explanations.A: Correct. With 50/50 play the game is symmetric and zero-sum, so each player's expected payoff is exactly 0.
explanations.B: A payoff of 1 for Row would mean Row wins on average, impossible at the symmetric equilibrium.
explanations.C: Likewise Column cannot expect +1 against an equal mixer.
explanations.D: Expected payoffs are not positive for either side; they net to zero.

questions.question: In a first-price auction with independent private values, rational bidders typically:
options: shade bids below their true value
bid exactly their true value always

explanations.A: Correct. You pay your bid, so you shade below your value to leave positive expected surplus, trading some win probability for profit when you win.
explanations.B: Bidding your true value wins you zero surplus, so it is not optimal in a first-price format.
explanations.C: Bidding above value risks paying more than the item is worth — a guaranteed loss when you win.
explanations.D: Never bidding forfeits all expected surplus; the optimal bid is positive but shaded.

remediation.question: Bid shading reduces the risk of:

explanations.A: Correct. Shading lowers the price you pay when you win, reducing the chance of winning with little or negative surplus.
explanations.B: Shading reduces overpayment, not your ability to ever win.
explanations.C: Shading is a bidding tactic, not a reason to stop participating.
explanations.D: Shading does not depend on observing others' private values.

remediation.question: If everyone bids true value in a first-price auction, you might win when:
options: your value barely exceeds others' bids, leaving no surplus

explanations.A: Correct. Bidding full value means you win only by a razor-thin margin and pay nearly your whole value, leaving essentially no surplus.
explanations.B: Bidding true value does not make you always lose; it just erodes your surplus when you win.
explanations.C: First-price auctions are not inherently zero-sum among bidders.
explanations.D: The scenario is first-price; the second-price rule is what makes truthful bidding optimal.

questions.question: In matching pennies, Row wins if both players show the same side (HH or TT); Column wins if they differ. In the unique mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium, each player randomizes:

explanations.A: Correct. Equal mixing makes the opponent indifferent between heads and tails, which is required for a mixed equilibrium.
explanations.B: Always heads is exploitable—Column would always play tails.
explanations.C: Unequal mixing lets the opponent exploit the predictable bias.
explanations.D: Any pure strategy is a best response target for the other player.

remediation.question: Why must a player randomize in matching pennies?
options: Any predictable pattern can be exploited by the opponent
The game has a dominant pure strategy

explanations.A: Correct. Pure strategies lose against a best-responding opponent.
explanations.B: Payoffs differ by outcome; that is why strategy matters.
explanations.C: There is no dominant pure strategy in matching pennies.
explanations.D: Mixed strategies are essential here.

remediation.question: In the mixed equilibrium of matching pennies, each player's expected payoff is:

explanations.A: Correct. Symmetric zero-sum mixing gives each player zero expected value.
explanations.B: 1 would mean Row always wins in expectation.
explanations.C: −1 would mean Row always loses in expectation.
explanations.D: 0.5 is not the equilibrium expected payoff here.

questions.question: Two traders choose Cooperate or Defect. Mutual cooperation yields (3, 3); mutual defection (1, 1); one defects alone (5, 0). Which is true?
options: Defect is a dominant strategy for both players
Cooperate is a dominant strategy for both players

explanations.A: Correct. Defect gives a higher payoff regardless of the other's action, so both defect in the unique Nash equilibrium.
explanations.B: Cooperate is never better when the other defects (0 vs 1).
explanations.C: (Defect, Defect) is a Nash equilibrium.
explanations.D: The game is a standard simultaneous-move payoff matrix.

remediation.question: A dominant strategy is one that:
options: Is best regardless of what the other player does
Is best only if the other cooperates

explanations.A: Correct. Dominance is defined against all opponent actions.
explanations.B: Conditional superiority is not dominance.
explanations.C: Dominant strategies are pure by definition here.
explanations.D: Many finite games have dominant strategies.

remediation.question: In the prisoners' dilemma, the Nash equilibrium outcome is:

explanations.A: Correct. Both would prefer (3,3) over (1,1), but defection is individually rational.
explanations.B: (1,1) is not Pareto optimal since cooperation improves both.
explanations.C: Equilibrium is mutual defection, not cooperation.
explanations.D: The equilibrium is well defined.

placementQuestions.question: Nash equilibrium: no player gains by:

explanations.A: Correct. At a Nash equilibrium no player can improve their payoff by deviating alone.
explanations.B: Cooperation may or may not help; the defining feature is no profitable unilateral deviation.
explanations.C: Randomizing can be part of an equilibrium, not what is ruled out.
explanations.D: Observing payoffs is just information, not a deviation that is blocked.

explanations.A: Correct. A dominant strategy gives the best payoff no matter what opponents do.
explanations.B: Being best only under cooperation is conditional, not dominant.
explanations.C: Dominant strategies do exist (for example, defect in the prisoner's dilemma).
explanations.D: Dominance is about pure best responses across all opponent moves, not specific to mixed equilibria.

placementQuestions.question: Zero-sum: one player's gain is the other's:

explanations.A: Correct. Zero-sum means payoffs are a pure transfer — one's gain equals the other's loss.
explanations.B: If both gained, the sum would not be zero.
explanations.C: The transfer is exact, not random.
explanations.D: Zero-sum games are competitive, not cooperative.

explanations.A: Correct. Defect dominates for both, so mutual defection is the unique Nash equilibrium.
explanations.B: Mutual cooperation is better jointly but unstable to unilateral defection.
explanations.C: One-of-each is not stable; the cooperator would switch to defect.
explanations.D: An equilibrium exists — mutual defection.

placementQuestions.question: Matching pennies equilibrium mixes heads at:

explanations.A: Correct. Equal 50/50 mixing makes the opponent indifferent and cannot be exploited.
explanations.B: Probability 1 (always heads) is an exploitable pure strategy.
explanations.C: Probability 0 (never heads) is equally exploitable.
explanations.D: 1/4 leaves a predictable bias; the equilibrium is exactly 1/2.

placementQuestions.question: Mixed equilibrium requires equal expected payoff across:

explanations.A: Correct. A player mixes only when the actions they randomize over yield equal expected payoff.
explanations.B: Different players can have different equilibrium payoffs.
explanations.C: Mixed equilibria arise in many games, not only zero-sum ones.
explanations.D: The condition concerns a player's own mixed actions, not cooperation.

explanations.A: Correct. Backward induction reasons from the final decisions back to the first move.
explanations.B: 'Always defect' is a prisoner's-dilemma result, not a method for sequential games.
explanations.C: Random play ignores the sequential structure.
explanations.D: Ignoring later moves discards the information backward induction relies on.

explanations.A: Correct. In a first-price auction the winner pays exactly their own bid.
explanations.B: Paying the second-highest bid is the second-price (Vickrey) rule.
explanations.C: The winner does pay; the auction is not free.
explanations.D: Winners pay their own bid, not an average.
